title: Slab box with lid in air-dry clay
subtitle: A rectangular box built from rolled slabs with a fitting lid
excerpt: A slab box is built from five flat sheets of clay: four walls and a base, with a sixth slab forming the lid. The geometry is straightforward but the joins require care to keep the corners square and the lid fitting. No kiln or wheel is needed. Air-dry clay produces a decorative box suitable for jewellery, keys, or keepsakes.
sourceNotes: Slab-building technique. Encyclopaedia Britannica, 11th edition (1911), ceramics entry. Technique derived from first principles.

content.text: A
slab

box is one of the most practical hand-building forms: a container with square corners, a flat base, and a fitting lid. No kiln is needed. Air-dry clay produces a decorative box that holds small objects well. The main skill is getting the corners square and the lid sized correctly so it sits without rattling or jamming. Plan for a two-day build: the walls are assembled on day one and left to stiffen, the lid is measured and fitted on day two.

attrs.body: Measure the opening for the lid only after the box walls are at leather-hard: clay shrinks as it dries, and a lid cut at the wet stage will be too large once both have dried. Cut the lid slightly oversize and trim to fit.
attrs.title: Sizing the lid

content.text: What you need

items.name: Pottery rolling pin and 8 mm guide rails

content.text: Building the box
Step 1: Roll the slab and cut the panels

Roll the clay to an even 8 mm thick. For a box 12 cm long, 9 cm wide, and 6 cm tall, cut: one base piece 12 x 9 cm, two long walls 12 x 6 cm, two short walls 9 x 6 cm. Cut cleanly against a metal ruler with the fettling knife. Leave the pieces on the canvas board, covered loosely with plastic, until they reach
leather-hard
: firm enough to stand upright without bending.
Step 2: Assemble the walls on the base
Use
score-and-slip

to join each wall to the base. Score the bottom edge of each wall panel and the corresponding edge of the base. Apply slurry, press firmly, and hold for 15 seconds. Stand the long walls first, then fit the short walls inside them to fill the corners. Blend every interior join with a finger, pushing clay from the wall down into the base at each corner.
Step 3: Join the corners

Score the touching side edges of each pair of adjacent walls and apply slurry. Press the corners together and blend the interior seams with a finger from top to bottom in a single smooth stroke. Reinforce each corner with a small coil of fresh clay pressed into the interior angle and blended flat. This is the most load-bearing join in the box.
Step 4: Leave to stiffen overnight

Cover the box with a loose plastic bag and leave overnight. Do not try to move or measure for the lid until the box has stiffened further. A box that is moved while the joins are still fresh will rack: the corners pull out of square and the lid will not fit.
Step 5: Cut and fit the lid

Measure the opening of the box with a ruler. Roll a fresh slab 6 mm thick and cut a piece 3 mm larger on each side than the measured opening: this oversize allows for trimming to a perfect fit. Set the lid panel on top of the box while both are at matching leather-hard stage and trim the edges until the lid drops squarely into place. Add a simple knob: roll a ball 2 cm across, flatten one face, score both the flat face and the lid centre, slurry, and press firmly.
Drying and finishing

Dry the box and lid separately to avoid them bonding together. Allow two full days for bone-dry. Sand any rough corners lightly with 120-grit sandpaper outdoors or with a mask. Seal with PVA diluted 1:1 with water for a semi-matte surface, or with an acrylic varnish for a harder finish.
Variations
Cut a decorative edge on the lid: a wavy or scalloped edge gives the box a more sculptural quality.
Impress texture into the wall panels before assembly: stamps, leaves, or lace pressed into the slab at the rolling stage.
Use paper clay for better join strength at the corners, particularly for boxes taller than 8 cm.
Common problems

attrs.intro: Problems in slab-box building and how to address them.

items.fix: Score the cracked area, apply fresh slurry, press a small clay coil into the crack on the inside, and blend flat. Dry slowly under plastic.
items.cause: The interior corner reinforcement was not applied, or joins were not blended thoroughly.

items.fix: Sand the lid edges with 120-grit sandpaper to reduce the size gradually.
items.cause: Lid was cut to match the wet-stage opening before both pieces had dried and shrunk.
items.symptom: Lid is too large after drying

items.fix: On future builds, leave undisturbed overnight before any handling. For a racked box, gently press the corners back into square while still leather-hard.
items.cause: Box was moved or the lid was placed while the joins were still fresh.

attrs.heading: Common problems
